peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness, dutifulness, and system justification support accepting electoral outcomes, though a history of legal system contact and moderate external efficacy introduce slight skepticism.
Strong support for democratic norms due to high trust in institutions and low collective narcissism, but moderate system justification tempers extreme endorsement.

Strong respect for rules (justice value) and high dutifulness (99.78) support procedural acceptance, yet high collective narcissism (94.21) and a dangerous worldview (79.16) create some reluctance to concede to out-groups.
Moderate support arises from very high agreeableness and fairness values, but weak external political efficacy and a competitive worldview create ambivalence.

Strong authoritarian submission (very low) and social dominance orientation (low) are countered by high competitive jungle worldview and moderate ethnic nationalism, leading to mild ambivalence about conceding to political opponents.

Strong civic nationalism (75.78) and high agreeableness (95.64) support fair-play norms, while very low system justification (5.37) and low RWA (19.7) reduce deference to winners, yielding clear but not maximal endorsement.
Weak support due to low political trust (external efficacy 18.45) and high system justification (92.88) creating a grudging acceptance of procedural norms despite low faith in fairness.
Strong fairness/equity values (0.75) and high internal political efficacy (89.34) support loser consent, though high collective narcissism (89.11) introduces some resistance.
